slides.title: outbox-md
slides.subtitle: Architecture & vision — a friendly deep dive

slides.title: The mental model
bullets: Comments aren't edits — they're commands in a queue
The .md file is just a projection of the latest version
The AI is the processor that drains the queue
Smells like event sourcing? Yep — basically

slides.title: A substrate, not an AI client
One round of the loop
steps: Agent claims it with a lease

slides.title: Why your doc can't get corrupted
bullets: Applies are strictly ordered (compare-and-swap)
The outbox is durable — survives crashes
The file write is atomic, with rollback
Nothing you wrote is ever silently lost

slides.title: Anchoring — the genuinely hard part
bullets: Your comment is pinned to a range of text
Then the AI rewrites the whole document
We diff old → new and slide every anchor to follow it
If the text is gone, the comment detaches cleanly

slides.title: Bring your own agent (MCP)
bullets: No API keys, no model baked in
The MCP boundary is the only seam an agent touches
Claude, GPT, Cursor — or whatever's next
The AI council = just more agents on the same wire

slides.title: It grows up: governance
bullets: draft → in-review → approved baseline
After approval, changes are tracked amendments
Every comment carries provenance — who, and why
Specs link to specs — a graph, not a pile of files

slides.title: A living, governed knowledge graph of specs that multiple AIs critique and improve.
slides.subtitle: Fully ordered. Fully audited. Fully retrievable.

slides.title: Come build the fun parts
bullets: The seams are already designed in
Pick a layer: council · dashboard · retrieval
MIT-licensed, DCO-signed commits
Good first issues are waiting

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Okay, we did the intro. Now I want the real thing — how does outbox-md actually work under the hood?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Ha, alright — let's pop the hood. Grab a coffee, this is the fun part.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Give me the one big idea first.

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: The trick is to stop thinking of feedback as edits. Your comments are commands that go into a queue. The Markdown file is just a projection of the latest version, and the AI is the processor that drains the queue. If that smells like event sourcing — yeah, basically.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: So where does the AI actually live in this picture?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Outside of it, on purpose. outbox-md is one Go binary that holds your docs, the queue, and the history, and exposes all of it over MCP. The browser is your review UI, and any agent connects from the side.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Wait — zero keys in the app itself? So it's not really an AI app. It's more like the table the AIs sit around.

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Exactly. It's a substrate, not an AI client. Bring whatever brain you like.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Walk me through one round.

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: You highlight text and drop a comment — it lands in the outbox, durably. An agent claims it with a lease, proposes a change, and you accept. Then the careful step: we advance the version with a compare-and-swap, write the file atomically, and re-anchor every other comment.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: That compare-and-swap bit — why so careful?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Because the one promise we can never break is: don't corrupt your document. So applies are strictly ordered, the queue survives crashes, the file write is atomic with a rollback, and nothing you wrote is ever silently lost. Boring guarantees — but that's the whole point.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: What was the hardest part to actually get right?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Anchoring, easily. Your comment is pinned to a range of text — then the AI rewrites the whole document. We diff the old and new versions and slide every anchor to wherever its text moved. And if the text is just gone, the comment detaches cleanly instead of pointing at nonsense.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: And this MCP thing is what makes it agent-agnostic?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Right. The MCP boundary is the only seam an agent ever touches. That's why the AI council — multiple models voting on a change — isn't some big new subsystem. It's just more agents on the same wire.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Okay, but a toy that edits one file is one thing. Does this scale to real spec work?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: That's where it grows up. A doc moves from draft, to reviewed, to an approved baseline. After approval, changes become tracked amendments that need re-approval. Every comment carries provenance, and specs can link to other specs — so you get a graph, not a pile of files.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: So what's actually built versus where's it going?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Today, version one is the walking skeleton — the whole loop, end to end. Next is the full editor, approval, and a dashboard. Then the council. And then the really fun stuff: a spec graph where an agent can flag that a change to one spec breaks another, plus retrieval over the whole corpus.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Paint me the endgame.

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: A living, governed knowledge graph of specs that multiple AIs critique and improve — fully ordered, fully audited, fully retrievable. Your specs stop being dead documents and start being a system.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Love it. If someone wants in, where do they start?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Honestly, the fun parts are wide open. The seams are already designed in — pick a layer: the council, the dashboard, retrieval. It's MIT, commits are DCO-signed, and there are good first issues waiting.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Alright — that's the deep dive. Come build with us.

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: The repo's in the description. See you in the pull requests.
